title: A Shortcut To Seclusion
content:
I am 5,000 feet up in New Zealand's Southern Alps when I realize I am completely surrounded by clouds. Delicate wisps float up from the valley below, curling around craggy peaks that stretch almost two miles high. Through the streaky veil, I can spot snow-crowned summits and alpine lakes in stunning shades of silty gray and sapphire. Waterfalls relentlessly pour down sheer rock faces. This breathtaking section of the Shotover Saddle Route marks the beginning of my hike.

Some people arrive here as part of a 10.5-mile trek that requires a 4,000-foot elevation gain and at least seven hours of strenuous effort. I, however, have come by helicopter. It feels a little bit like cheating. If hiking tends to be more about the journey than the destination, it is easy to write off heli-hiking. It is, after all, a mechanical shortcut that puts almost any epic vantage point within immediate reach.

But it is an established way to take on an adventure in New Zealand, where dramatically foreboding landscapes can preclude other means of transportation. It is also common in Canada, where the popularity of heli-skiing gave way to a summer equivalent in the 1980s. As a means of exploring, it is gaining in popularity globally. From the Himalayas to the Italian and Swiss Alps, travelers are increasingly climbing aboard choppers to experience breathtaking places. The concept makes hiking and nature itself accessible to people across a wide variety of ages and physical abilities.

This method is especially appealing to the cash-rich, time-poor set. With modern consumers seeking to pack more adventure into a tighter schedule, travel companies have started employing helicopters for all sorts of pursuits. Prices for these experiences range tremendously. Half-day heli-hiking jaunts into the Talkeetna Mountains of Alaska to ogle views of Denali run as little as  for two people. At Minaret Station, my hotel on the western shores of Lake Wanaka in New Zealand, the signature half-day heli-charter costs substantially more.

As an experienced hiker, I am usually drawn to the way traditional hiking pushes my physical limits and gets me into quiet places. Heli-hiking initially seems at odds with all of this. The loud helicopter rotor shatters the silence, and the leaned-back experience of sitting down during the main ascent feels unearned. But it also seems incredibly efficient, time-wise. In my 20s, I could afford to spend two weeks getting lost along the way to an off-the-grid cabin.

Now in my 40s, I would need to take a massive sabbatical for that kind of luxury. The option of surveying vastly different terrain over multiple hikes and returning each night to a cushy lodge felt like a happy medium. Perhaps adventure and exploration do not always have to be synonymous with suffering. Ultimately, this modern approach allowed me to witness alpine majesty that I otherwise might never have seen.
